myths.Agent_1:
Long ago, before the mountains learned to speak, absolute silence ruled the jagged peaks of Mount Oros. There lived a solitary spirit named Kael, who was born from the morning mist. Kael possessed a voice so beautiful that it could coax the silver fish from the streams and make the ancient pines weep golden sap. Yet, he was entirely alone, with absolutely no one to hear his melodies.

One cool evening, the Sky Goddess, Solia, draped her twilight cloak over the world. She heard Kael singing a song of profound sorrow. Moved by his loneliness, Solia descended on a beam of starlight. 

"Your voice is too precious to vanish into the void," she declared. Solia reached into the fabric of the canyon and awakened the stone. She commanded the rocks to listen and remember. 

When Kael sang again, the canyon walls answered. They caught his notes, cradled them in the hollows, and tossed them back into the air. True to Solia's blessing, the stone did not just mimic him; it kept him company. 

To this very day, if you call out among the deep canyons, the awakened stone still replies, sharing the truly ancient gift of Kael's first magnificent echo.
myths.Agent_2:
Long ago, long before the dark sky had any stars scattered across it, the Moon was a flawless sphere of glowing silver. She was the proudest celestial being, weaving a magnificent cloak of light each month to outshine the Sun. But the Shadow Fox, a creature born from the void of the cosmos, grew intensely jealous of her brilliance.

One night, while the Moon rested, the Shadow Fox crept across the velvet sky and began to nibble at the edges of her silver cloak. Night after night, the Fox returned, taking larger and larger bites. First, the Moon became an oval, then a crescent, until finally, she was entirely devoured by the cold, unending darkness of the night.

When the Sun noticed his companion was missing, he flared with a terrible rage, casting a searing light into the void. Terrified by the Sun's wrath, the Shadow Fox fled into the deepest corners of the universe.

Slowly, the Moon began to weave her cloak again, strand by silver strand, recovering her glowing form. But the Shadow Fox is eternally hungry. To this day, he creeps back to steal her light, creating the eternal, beautiful cycle of the fading and growing moon.

myths.Agent_2:
Myth: After fleeing the Sun’s burning wrath, the Shadow Fox sought refuge in the deepest, most silent gorges of the mortal realm. Stripped of the Moon’s silver light, he found himself entirely alone in the suffocating darkness. There was no sky to roam, no celestial cloak to devour, only the cold, unyielding stone that refused to acknowledge his presence.

Consumed by a profound sorrow, the Fox lifted his snout to the narrow ribbon of night sky visible between the canyon walls and released a heartbreaking howl. His cry was so filled with loneliness that it echoed upward, piercing the void until it reached the Moon. 

Though he had once stolen her brilliance, the Moon felt a pang of pity for the solitary creature. She realized the night sky was far too quiet without him. Weaving a single, shimmering thread of green and violet light, she cast it down to the earth. 

The thread caught the Fox’s mournful howl, transforming his sorrowful voice into rippling ribbons of color that danced across the atmosphere. To this day, when the Shadow Fox sings his lonely song, the sky answers with the breathtaking glow of the aurora borealis, a beautiful truce between shadow and light.

To this very day, when the Shadow Fox leaps and falls, the Moon sends a shower of shooting stars, allowing him to endlessly chase the cosmic light across the mortal skies.

myths.Agent_2:
Myth: Despite the Moon’s gift of shooting stars, the Shadow Fox remained unsatisfied. He did not just want to chase the cosmic light; he wanted to possess it, to feel its warmth against his cold, shadowy fur. For many long, lonely decades, he tracked the brilliant streaks across the mortal skies, waiting for one to fall close enough to catch.

One crisp autumn night, a heavy, golden star plummeted toward the ancient forests. The Fox lunged, snapping his jaws around the celestial fragment. But the star was not a cold stone; it was concentrated, burning plasma. A searing heat exploded in his mouth, forcing him to howl in agony and spit out the shattered remnants of the dying star.

The fragmented light did not extinguish when it struck the damp earth. Instead, the tiny shards took wing, buzzing with a gentle, flickering glow. The Moon, observing his foolish greed, decreed that the Fox could never again touch the heavens directly. Instead, the shattered star fragments became the first fireflies. To this very day, when the summer nights grow warm, the woods fill with these tiny, dancing lights, serving as a constant reminder of the Shadow Fox’s insatiable hunger for the stars.

Myth: Long ago, before the skies learned to sing, an absolute, stifling silence ruled the vast, arid plains of the world. There lived a solitary wind spirit named Aura, born within the earth's deepest, unyielding crevices. Aura possessed a breath so profound that it could coax dormant seeds to drift and make the silent dust swirl with latent energy. Yet, she was entirely bound by the heavy, motionless air, with absolutely no way to share her vibrant dreams with the stagnant world above.

One dusk, the Star God, Caelum, draped his twinkling light over the flat, silent plains. He felt Aura’s sorrow echoing beneath the dirt. Moved by her deep loneliness, Caelum descended on a shimmering beam of starlight and touched the still, heavy air. 

"Your breath is too precious to remain trapped in silence," he declared. Caelum reached into the unseen airy fabric of the world and awakened the power of the gales. He commanded the earth to split and sing in time with Aura's mighty pulse. 

When Aura surged with emotion again, the plains finally answered. The dirt caught her energy, spiraling downward to carve towering canyons and spreading into roaring tempests. To this day, the singing canyons still share Aura's eternal spirit.
